topic: How Aurora Borealis Happens With Solar Particles
title: Why the Northern Lights Appear When the Sun Disturbs Earth's Magnetosphere

lesson.learn: Aurora borealis happens when charged particles from the Sun are guided by Earth's magnetic field toward the polar atmosphere. Those particles collide with oxygen and nitrogen, which then release light as they relax. The exact colors depend on which gas is excited and how high above Earth the collision occurs.
lesson.simpler: Solar particles hit Earth's upper atmosphere near the poles and make the sky glow green, red, and sometimes purple.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Auroras are a visible result of solar particles interacting with Earth's magnetosphere and upper atmosphere.
lesson.deeperDive: Auroras become more frequent and more widespread during periods of high solar activity, especially when coronal mass ejections disturb Earth's magnetic environment. The auroral oval can expand toward lower latitudes during strong geomagnetic storms. Scientists use auroras as a useful tracer of how energy moves from the Sun into near-Earth space. In that sense, the lights are beautiful, but they are also a diagnostic sign of space-weather activity.
lesson.funFact: The same particle energy that lights up auroras can also produce faint glows in the skies of other planets with magnetic fields.

people: Space weather researchers - modern aurora analysts
places: Auroral oval - the main aurora zone around the poles
The upper atmosphere - where light is emitted
High-latitude observing sites - best aurora viewing
years: 2024 - major geomagnetic storms push auroras south
